pages.full_text:
[Text continues from previous page] became Christian, performances stopped.
[Later] A wall called the pulpitum separated the orchestra from the stage. The exhibits were a well [illegible] more, including a virtual reality [?Silex] where we wore headsets. The Romans enjoyed Tragedies & Comedies. Operas are still staged there today.
We had a meal - fish burghers - & visited the

[Photo caption, next to large aerial photograph of the Roman theatre and town:]
The proscenium wall & the Theatre - Louis XIV said the finest wall of the kingdom [H]ere. The Theatre was built into St Eutrope's hill (only big towns like Rome could afford to build on the flat).
photo_captions: The proscenium wall & the Theatre - Louis XIV said the finest wall of the kingdom [H]ere. The Theatre was built into St Eutrope's hill (only big towns like Rome could afford to build on the flat).
pages.notes: Page begins mid-sentence continuing from B_0328_R which ended 'Once Roma[n]'. The page is dominated by a large colour aerial photograph of what appears to be Orange (the Roman Theatre is visible in the photo). The caption text is written alongside/around the photo on the right side. The word after 'well' before 'more' is illegible — possibly 'done' or 'made' or a word meaning 'curated'. '[?Silex]' is uncertain — could be a brand name for the virtual reality equipment. 'fish burghers' is verbatim — possibly means 'fish burgers'. Handwritten page number 30 inferred from sequence (B_0328_R was page 29); the top-left corner number is partially obscured by the photo/scan edge. The right-hand margin of this image shows partial overlap text from the right page.

pages.full_text:
museum. It holds an amazing marble outline 3 of them, ABC, of surveys done in 70 & 200 AD & later, showing who owned which parcel of land, who rented which. After being subdued, the land was parcelled out to old soldiers... sometimes land (the poorer land) was returned to the natives [?C. Sackd]
	It was the old soldiers who raised the Triumphal Arch in memory of their commanders.
	Back at the campsite we rested & swam & went on another bike ride, this time in the countryside. The man at the pool insisted I shower, even though I'd just had one. Simon doesn't like the look of the bar. There's a singer this evening. Simon was asleep in 30 secs. I lay awake 2h[s].
	The camp has lanes called Voie Ganius, Voie Augustes ... unfortunately we are just off Voie Caligula. 28° in the day.
	__Sunday__
	Hot hot sunshine at breakfast. We set off north. We have stopped for elevenses in an Aire called 45° - it's midway between the North Pole & the Equator, V. far down in France. The lavender has come out during our 2 weeks down here. (Bham is about 5[illegible])
	We drove about 6 hours today. Lunch was on an
pages.notes: Page continues directly from B_0329_L ('& visited the / museum.'). Handwritten page number at top right appears to be 31 (partially visible; inferred from sequence). '[?C. Sackd]' is a small annotation after 'the natives' — unclear, possibly an abbreviation or reference. 'Voie Ganius' could alternatively read 'Voie Gaius' — the Roman name Gaius/Caius is plausible for a Roman-themed campsite lane at Orange; 'Voie Augustes' likely refers to Augustus. 'Voie Caligula' is verbatim. 'Bham' is abbreviation for Birmingham. 'Aire called 45°' refers to a French motorway rest stop (aire de service) positioned at 45 degrees latitude. Text ends mid-sentence ('Lunch was on an') — continues on the next spread. No year is explicit on this page; the trip to southern France with lavender in bloom and Orange (Roman ruins) context is consistent with a summer trip; year cannot be determined from this page alone.
